coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers and carbon dots for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Hanmeng
Peng, Junwen
Ren, Tingting
Luo, Qiuyan
Luo, Yong
Zhang, Ning
Huang, Yong
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers and carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized for the high-value utilization of lignin and control of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). Its chemical and physical structure was characterized, and its Cr(VI) sorption performance was evaluated. The results demonstrated that 3D porous structures were constructed in this hydrogel. The maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel was 599.9 mg/g, and its sorption performance met Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models. Meanwhile, this novel hydrogel exhibited high sensitivity to Cr(VI), with a limit of detection of 11.2 mg/L and a wide linear range from 15 to 200 mg/L. Moreover, its mechanism for efficiently adsorbing and detecting Cr(VI) was analyzed. The results confirmed that the efficient adsorption and detection were due to these 3D porous structures generated by the lignin and cellulose nanofibers modified with CDs. The porous structures provided many active sites and ion transport channels, thereby improving the adsorption, and stabilized the fluorescence signal, thus enhancing the detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon dots
Adsorption
Detection
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of this novel hydrogel.

Characterization of the novel hydrogel. (a) SEM image; (b) optical microscopy image; (c) XRD spectra; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) UV–vis and PL spectra; (f) This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior; (g) The fluorescence appearances of the prepared lignin-based hydrogels.

(a) Heavy metal sorption process; (b) Cr(VI) sorption data of FLH-3 and OLH-0 at different initial concentrations; (c) The effect of time on the adsorption; (d) Langmuir isotherm result; (e) Freundlich isotherm result; (f) Fitting results of pseudo-first-order model; (g) Fitting results of pseudo-second-order model; (h) Fluorescent behaviours of the lignin-based hydrogel after the addition of different metal ions at the concentration of 2000 mg/L; (i) Variations of AQY of the lignin-based hydrogel FLH-3 caused by adding various heavy metals; (j) The gradual fluorescence decrease of FLH-3 with the increase of the Cr(VI) concentration.
(a) FTIR spectra before and after sorption; (b) Total XPS survey scans of FLH-3; (c, d and e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Cr2p, O1s and N1s.
The hydrogel's mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI).
Adsorption capacities in comparison with previous work.

row.entry: PAamGG (polyacrylamide/guar gum graft copolymer) crosslinked hydrogel
Sponge-like chitosan (CS)/reduced grapheme oxide (rGO)/montmorillonite (MT) composite hydrogels
Macroporous Poly(N, N0-dimethylamino ethyl methacrylate) Hydrogels

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers and carbon dots for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central South University of Forestry and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint International Research Laboratory of Biomass Energy and Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers and carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized for the high-value utilization of lignin and control of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). Its chemical and physical structure was characterized, and its Cr(VI) sorption performance was evaluated. The results demonstrated that 3D porous structures were constructed in this hydrogel. The maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel was 599.9 mg/g, and its sorption performance met Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models. Meanwhile, this novel hydrogel exhibited high sensitivity to Cr(VI), with a limit of detection of 11.2 mg/L and a wide linear range from 15 to 200 mg/L. Moreover, its mechanism for efficiently adsorbing and detecting Cr(VI) was analyzed. The results confirmed that the efficient adsorption and detection were due to these 3D porous structures generated by the lignin and cellulose nanofibers modified with CDs. The porous structures provided many active sites and ion transport channels, thereby improving the adsorption, and stabilized the fluorescence signal, thus enhancing the detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin is the most abundant natural aromatic polymer produced in the secondary cell walls of vascular plants (Chio et al., 2019; Hess et al., 2018; Lan and Luterbacher, 2019). The mass content of lignin in vascular plants is from 15% to 30%, and several million tons of lignin is synthesized by plants around the world each year. Approximately 70 million tons of lignin is extracted as a by-product of the global pulp and papermaking industry (Zhang et al., 2017). Most of the extracted lignin (above 95%) is burned, converted to energy, or released directly into the environment (Forsberg, 2010; Sameni et al., 2013; Yiamsawas et al., 2017). These unreasonable treatments of lignin cause not only economic waste but also serious environmental pollution. Hence, developing a new way to achieve high-value utilization of lignin offers great economic and environmental benefits.

Lignin-based hydrogels have recently attracted considerable attention for water purification via adsorption (Ma et al., 2019; Thakur et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2018). As lignin contains many phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which have electrostatic interactions with heavy metals (Sun et al., 2019), the application of lignin in hydrogel synthesis can not only improve the adsorption properties of hydrogel but also achieve high-value utilization of lignin. Kwak et al. (2016) prepared a silk sericin/lignin blend to remove hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) ions from aqueous solutions. Li et al. (2016) fabricated a lignosulfonate-based graphene hydrogel using a hydrothermal method as a Pb(II) adsorbent. Wang et al. (2016) prepared a cellulose/lignin composite hydrogel for absorbing Pb(II) ions, and its maximum sorption capacity was measured. These studies showed that the adsorption properties of lignin hydrogels could be improved by mixing other preferable materials.

To further improve the performance of lignin-based hydrogels, carboxylated cellulose nanofibrils (CCNs) and carbon dots (CDs) can be introduced. CCNs, a kind of biomass material, may find application in hydrogel systems. Due to their inherent characteristics, such as biodegradability, easy-to-form network structures, and rich bonding sites for heavy metals, CCNs can help achieve good performance. Moreover, CDs are widely used as fluorescent probes due to their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity (Gong et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2013). The good fluorescence properties show great potential for trace analyses of metal ions (e.g., Cr6+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) (Elavarasi et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2017). Therefore, the motivation of this work is to incorporate CCNs and CDs into a lignin-based hydrogel to add new functionality and improve performance.

Lignin-based hydrogels can be used in water treatment to remove heavy metals (Meng et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2016; F. Zhou et al., 2018). Among these heavy metals, chromium is a key environmental contaminant generated from many industrial processes (Fan et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2020). Cr(VI) is highly toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic (Fan et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2017). Therefore, designing and fabricating a novel lignin-based hydrogel for the removal of Cr(VI) will be meaningful.

In the present study, a fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel was synthesized and characterized. The Cr(VI) sorption behavior of this hydrogel was investigated, in which the effects of the initial Cr(VI) concentration and contact time were analyzed. This hydrogel was further used as a solid-state fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI). Finally, its mechanism of highly efficient adsorption and detection is proposed.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid (CA), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), acrylic acid (AA), N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), alkali lignin (AL) and potassium persulfate (PPS) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company.

The preparation procedure of CCNs has been reported, and we used the same procedure here (Guo et al., 2018). First, 10 g of dried bleached wood pulp was dispersed in 350 mL of distilled water. NaBr (0.4 g), NaClO (6.0 g), and TEMPO (0.08 g) were added in turn under mechanical stirring. Once the pH value of the mixture became 11, the oxidation reaction began. Six hours later, 60 mL of ethanol was added to stop the reaction. Subsequently, the mixture was washed with distilled water to neutral pH and then passed through a high-pressure homogenizer six times. Next, the CCNs were prepared and dialyzed for four days. Finally, the CCNs were diluted to 1.0 wt%, and the carboxyl content of the obtained CCNs was measured to be 0.67 mmol/g by conductometric titration.

The CDs were synthesized according to a reported method (S. Zhu et al., 2013). First, 17 μL of EDA was added into 5 mL of a CA solution (0.10 g/mL) through stirring at 293 K for 2 h. Afterward, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined pressure vessel (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction completed and the mixture naturally cooled down to room temperature, a resulting dark brown solution was obtained. The product was filtered by a 0.22-μm microporous filter to remove large particles and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane for five days. Then, the CDs (0.1 wt%) were synthesized.

CCN–CD was prepared via a condensation reaction (Wu et al., 2019). First, 20 g of CCNs (1.0 wt%) was added into 5 mL of an acetate buffer (PH = 3.5) with 2 h of vigorous stirring. Second, 60 mg of EDC, 230 mg of NHS, and 15 mg of the CDs were added through 1 h of magnetic stirring for complete dissolution. Third, the reaction was conducted for 12 h at room temperature and protected from light. The CCN–CD was obtained and dialyzed against distilled water for four days. Finally, the CCN–CD (1.0 wt%) was stored for the next step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: This novel hydrogel was synthesized using free-radical polymerization. Initially, different contents of CCN–CD (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 g) were each continuously stirred at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, AA (0.5 g), NaOH (0.05 g), AL water solution (1.0 g, 1.0 wt%), MBA (0.03 g), and PPS (0.01 g) were added to CCN–CD at 40 °C with continuous stirring under a nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was heated to 65 °C and maintained for 12 h to complete the gelation process and obtain the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel. This hydrogel was submerged in water for four days to remove the residual reagents. The hydrogels were named FLH-1, FLH-2, FLH-3, FLH-4, FLH-5, and FLH-6.

Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis procedure of this hydrogel. By comparison with the original lignin hydrogel, the proposed hydrogel contains CCNs and CDs as important components. First, TEMPO oxidization and high-pressure homogenization were employed to synthesize the CCNs, and a hydrothermal reaction was used to prepare the CDs. Second, with CCNs and CDs as the raw materials, CCN–CD was formed through a condensation reaction. Finally, free-radical polymerization could be further performed on AL, CCN–CD, AA, and MBA to prepare the novel hydrogel with the help of an initiator.

Batch adsorption experiments were conducted on the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel FLH-3 and original lignin-based hydrogel, OLH-0, to explore the prepared hydrogel's adsorption properties. The effects of the initial Cr(VI) concentration and contact time were systemically investigated. The initial Cr(VI) concentration was set to 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mg/L, and the contact time was set to 5, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, and 200 min. The final equilibrium concentration was measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Optima 8300, PerkinElmer, USA). All the experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the means of the data were used in the following analysis.

An S-4800 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of the prepared hydrogel. The crystal structure was obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using IN 10™ Infrared Fourier Transform spectrometer (Nicolet Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by applying a VG Scientific Sigma Probe spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer and an Edinburgh Instruments FLS9800 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The quantum yield was calculated in absolute mode by comparing the number of emitted photons with the number of absorbed photons. The absolute quantum yield (AQY) was measured using the HAMAMATSU absolute quantum yield spectrometer C9920-02G including the AQY software. This accessory consists of a 3.3-inch integrating sphere which was placed into the spectrophotometer with its center at the crossing point of the excitation and detection light paths. A measurement of an empty quartz plate was required to get the full amount of excitation light which was reflected by a non-absorbing sample. For the measurements, the excitation wavelength was set to 437 nm and spectral corrections were used concerning the instrumental response and the integrating sphere. For the AQY calculation, the excitation wavelengths 425–447 nm and the emission wavelengths 465–734 nm were considered.

The porous structures and surface morphology of the novel hydrogel are shown by the SEM image (Fig. 2a) and optical microscopy image (Fig. 2b). Interconnected porous structures with different sizes were distributed heterogeneously. Interestingly, a small quantity of CCN–CD sized 1.27 ± 0.80 μm in diameter could be seen in the pore cavities. Hence, many active sites and ion transport channels could be provided for heavy metals, and the sorption capacity and rate of this hydrogel would be improved.

The CCN–CD, AL, and FLH-3 were characterized by XRD (Fig. 2c). CCN–CD showed three evident bands (22.6°, 16.2°, and 14.9°), which corresponded to the crystalline structure of cellulose I. Meanwhile, AL showed a significant dispersion peak, which was consistent with previous reports (Su et al., 2013; Su and Fang, 2014). In the case of FLH-3, these main characteristic bands cannot be observed, possibly due to the participation of CCN–CD and AL in the free-radical polymerization, causing the destruction of the original crystal structures.

The functional groups of CCNs, CCN–CD, AL, and FLH-3 were measured by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). The broad band ranging from 3500 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1 corresponded to the hydroxyl group. In the cases of the CCNs and CCN–CD, the peak at approximately 1056 cm−1 was assigned to the C–O stretching and ring vibrational modes of the cellulose species (Celino et al., 2014). For CCN–CD, a new peak emerged at 1648 cm−1 and was assigned to the stretching vibration of –CO–NH–, meaning that the CDs were crosslinked with the CCNs. In the case of AL, the peak at 1420 cm−1 was assigned to C–H bonds of the methyl group (Morales et al., 2020). In the case of FLH-3, the band at 1056 cm−1 associated with CCN–CD was not observed, and the band at 1420 cm−1 associated with AL shifted to 1406 cm−1; these findings were attributed to the participation of CCN–CD and AL in the free-radical polymerization.

The UV–vis spectra of FLH-3 are provided in Fig. 2e. Two evident absorption peaks of FLH-3 centered at 258 and 354 nm were observed. The absorption band at 258 nm originated from AL, and the band at 354 nm was assigned to the n-π* transition of the CDs (El-Zawawy, 2005; Omidi et al., 2017). Fig. 2e also illustrates the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of FLH-3. An optimal excitation band (437 nm) and emission band (515 nm) were found. When λ
ex moved from 377 nm to 497 nm, the λ
em of this novel hydrogel showed a bathochromic shift from 499 nm to 549 nm (Fig. 2f). This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior resulted from the surface state, which affected the band gap of the CDs.

The prepared hydrogel showed blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). The fluorescence appearances of FLH-1, FLH-2, FLH-3, FLH-4, FLH-5, and FLH-6 are illustrated in Fig. 2g. The fluorescence appearances were different, and FLH-3 showed the strongest fluorescence. The AQY of FLH-3 was 7.30%.

Fig. 3a visualizes the heavy metal sorption process. First, this novel hydrogel sample (radius: 1.8 cm, thickness: 1.0 cm) was completely submerged in water. Next, 0.4 g of copper sulfate was added with magnetic stirring for 5 min. After 1 h, the color of the solution lightened. Therefore, the novel hydrogel was an effective adsorbent for the removal of Cr(VI) ions.

The Cr(VI) sorption data of FLH-3 and OLH-0 at different initial concentrations are presented in Fig. 3b. The adsorption capacities of FLH-3 and OLH-0 increased with the Cr(VI) concentration. All equilibrium sorption capacities of FLH-3 were greater than those of OLH-0, which meant that the introduction of CCN–CD improved the adsorption ability of this novel hydrogel. Moreover, the Cr(VI) adsorption data of FLH-3 were fitted using isotherms (Fig. 3d and e). From the fitting results, the Freundlich isotherm result had a higher coefficient of determination (0.99355) than that of the Langmuir model (0.99162), but the difference was not evident. In the Langmuir adsorption model, the maximum sorption capacity of FLH-3 reached 599.9 mg/g (G. Zhou et al., 2018). The maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel was compared with those of other lignin-based hydrogels, as shown in Table 1 . This novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel was found to be an effective adsorbent for the removal of Cr(VI) ions because of its relatively large adsorption capacity.

Fig. 3c reveals the effect of time on the adsorption. For FLH-3, the sorption of Cr(VI) increased rapidly in the first 90 min, reaching about 90% of the maximum adsorption amount. Correspondingly, the sorption of OLH-0 could only increase rapidly in the beginning of the 160-min contact time. By contrast, FLH-3 had a higher adsorption speed. Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to fit with the experimental data of FLH-3 (Fig. 3f and g), and the fitted kinetic parameters were listed. The pseudo-second-order model agreed better with the experimental data, with higher correlation coefficients. The findings verified that the sorption kinetics of Cr(VI) on this novel hydrogel was well described by the pseudo-second-order equation.

The performance of this novel lignin-based hydrogel as a fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) detection was evaluated. First, the selectivity of the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel FLH-3 was investigated by evaluating the response of the method toward different metal ions, namely, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, Li+, Co2+, Al3+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, and Hg2+, at a concentration of 2000 mg/L. Figs. S1 and S2 show that only the Cr(VI) ions cause clear fluorescence quenching. Even with the interference of the other metals at the concentration of 2000 mg/L, the fluorescence quenching caused by the Cr(VI) ions remained the same (Figs. S3 and S4). In comparison with the weak influence on the fluorescent behavior for Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, and Ca2+, Cr(VI) exhibited complete fluorescence quenching for FLH-3 (Fig. 3h and i). Thus, FLH-3 exhibited high selectivity for Cr(VI) detection.

Cr(VI) standard solutions with different concentrations were mixed with FLH-3 to evaluate the sensitivity of the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel. Fig. 3j shows the gradual fluorescence quenching of FLH-3 with the increase of the Cr(VI) concentration. There was a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.99356) between the AQY of FLH-3 and the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range of 15–200 mg/L. The limit of detection (LOD) of FLH-3 for the Cr(VI) ions was estimated to be 11.2 mg/L at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Furthermore, the detection performance (linear range, LOD) of FLH-3 was compared with those in previous works (Table S1). This novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel has a wider linear range and higher detection limit, which may be due to its high adsorption capacity.

The feasibility of using the prepared lignin-based hydrogel in the detection of real water samples was explored in tap and pond water samples collected from the campus of Central South University of Forestry and Technology. Since the tap and pond water samples may not contain Cr(VI), the standard solutions containing different concentrations of Cr(VI) were added to the samples to investigate the accuracy of the detection. As shown in Table S2, the detected concentrations in the tap water and pond water based on the prepared lignin-based hydrogel were close to these spiked concentrations. The recoveries at the concentration range were high (from 90.51% to 103.93%), and the relative standard deviations were low (from 0.98% to 3.36%).

The adsorption and detection mechanism of the fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel was analyzed. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 4a), the peak of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations was at 3367 cm−1, and the band of –COO−stretching vibration was at 1560 cm−1. After sorption, these two bands shifted to 3338 and 1556 cm−1, respectively. Meanwhile, one new peak at 902 cm−1 was associated with the chromium species appeared. Hence, amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups were active sites for the Cr(VI) ions.

XPS analysis was applied for an in-depth investigation of the mechanism of FLH-3. In the total XPS survey scans of FLH-3 (Fig. 4b), in addition to the peaks of C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (399.8 eV), and O1s (531.4 eV), two peaks of Cr2p (579.7 and 588.9 eV) were found, indicating the adsorption of Cr(VI). Fig. 4c magnifies the Cr2p region at high resolution, where both Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 can be separated into two peaks; the ones at 579.7 and 576.6 eV were assigned to the Cr2p3/2 of Cr(VI) and Cr(III), while those at 588.9 and 586.6 eV were assigned to the Cr2p1/2 of Cr(VI) and Cr(III). Clearly, some Cr(VI) species could be reduced to Cr(III) ions. As shown in Fig. 4d, the high-resolution O1s spectra of FLH-3 indicated changes in C=O and C–O before and after sorption. The O1s region can be separated into two peaks: one at 532.3 eV (assigned to C=O) and the other at 531.1 eV (assigned to C–O). After sorption, the area ratio of these two peaks increased from 1.64 to 2.03, which suggested that the binding of chromium ions occurred at O atoms of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Fig. 4e presents the FLH-3 N1s spectra before and after sorption. No new peak could be found, and only a small shift of the N1s peak was observed. Thus, there was no formation of complexes between N and Cr.

On the basis of these results, the hydrogel's mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) was proposed (Fig. 5 ). First, due to the amino group originating from CD, the Cr(VI) ion was quickly adsorbed on the surface of this novel hydrogel by electrostatic attraction. In this hydrogel, CCNs were used as a natural frame to construct 3D porous structures that provide many ion transport channels and active sites for the Cr(VI) ion. With these 3D porous structures serving as ion transport channels, the Cr(VI) ion was quickly introduced from the surface to the inside. Then, part of the Cr(VI) ion could be reduced to Cr(III), the hydroxyl group was oxidized, and the reduced Cr(III) ion was adsorbed on this novel hydrogel. Due to these special structures, the hydrogel has excellent Cr(VI) adsorption ability. In addition, the CDs fixed to the 3D porous structures were applied to the detection of Cr(VI). Due to electrostatic attraction, the Cr(VI) ion was adsorbed on the surface of the CDs, thus leading to rapid fluorescence quenching. Given these unique structures, the prepared hydrogel stabilized the fluorescence signal and improved Cr(VI) detection sensitivity.

A novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel was synthesized by introducing CCN–CD. Batch adsorption experiments illustrated that the maximum sorption capacity of the novel hydrogel is as high as 599.9 mg/g. Moreover, this hydrogel exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity toward Cr(VI) in a wide linear range of 15 mg/L to 200 mg/L. Furthermore, XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed that the highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) are due to the 3D porous structures constructed by lignin and CCN–CD, which provide many ion transport channels and active sites and stabilize the fluorescence signal.

Hanmeng Yuan: Investigation. Junwen Peng: Software. Tingting Ren: Software. Qiuyan Luo: Investigation. Yong Luo: Supervision. Ning Zhang: Resources. Yong Huang: Resources. Xin Guo: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Validation. Yiqiang Wu: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project, National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31500475), Special Projects of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Hunan Forestry, China (No. XLK201982), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (No. 2019JJ50981) and Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province, China (No. 18A166).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.143395.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abdel-Halim and Al-Deyab, 2011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogel from crosslinked polyacrylamide/guar gum graft copolymer for sorption of hexavalent chromium ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Fan et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence sensing, electrochemical, and magenetic properties of 2D coordination polymers based on the mixed ligands p-terphenyl-2,2″,5″,5‴-tetracarboxylate acid and 1,10-phenanthroline

(2019) Luminescence sensing, electrochemical, and magenetic properties of 2D coordination polymers based on the mixed ligands p-terphenyl-2,2”,5”,5”'-tetracarboxylate acid and 1,10-phenanthroline.
